title: Old Testament Godhead Language (Faithlife Study Bible)
author: heiser

entries.title: The Angel of Yahweh as the Visible Second Yahweh
entries.chapter_or_episode: Israel's Binitarian Godhead — The Angel of Yahweh
entries.content_summary: The OT writers deliberately make the Angel of Yahweh indistinguishable from Yahweh (e.g., Exodus 3:1-14). In Exodus 23:20-23, the Angel has Yahweh's 'Name' in him — reference to 'the Name' actually refers to Yahweh Himself (Name theology). Yahweh indicates He IS in the Angel. Yet in other passages, Yahweh and the Angel are simultaneously but separately present (Judges 6). Various passages attribute Israel's deliverance from Egypt to both God and the Angel (Judges 2:1-3; 1 Sam 8:8; Micah 6:4). Genesis 48:15-16 fuses God and the Angel: Jacob blesses Joseph's sons by invoking 'The God... the God... the Angel who has redeemed me from all evil, may HE bless the boys' — the verb 'bless' is grammatically SINGULAR, denoting a tight fusion of the two divine beings.
entries.content_detail: The Angel that embodies Yahweh's presence parallels the role of Baal as co-regent who fights for El. In Joshua 5:13-15, the Angel leads Israel 'sword drawn in his hand' as captain of Yahweh's host. This description appears in only two other places, both referencing the Angel (Num 22:23; 1 Chr 21:16). Orthodox worship of Yahweh precluded cosmic rule by two separate deities, but COULD tolerate two personages of Yahweh. The Angel had the Presence (Name) of Yahweh in Him but was a distinct personage — He was Yahweh's presence, but not Him in His fullness.

entries.title: The Rider on the Clouds — Daniel 7:13 and the Second Yahweh
entries.chapter_or_episode: Israel's Binitarian Godhead — The Rider on the Clouds
entries.content_summary: The epithet 'Rider on the Clouds' was a well-known title for Baal in Canaanite religion. The Hebrew Bible consistently uses it and similar designations exclusively for Yahweh (Psalms 68:4; 68:33; 104:3; Deuteronomy 33:26; Isaiah 19:1) — with ONE exception: the 'son of man' in Daniel 7:13. This human figure is distinct from the Ancient of Days (the enthroned deity in Daniel 7) yet bears a title reserved exclusively for Yahweh in the OT. This is an unmistakable claim: the 'one like a son of man' IS a Yahweh figure — the second Yahweh, the visible one who comes in human form.
entries.content_detail: Orthodox Yahwism replaced the co-regent slot that Baal occupied with a binitarian Godhead in which Yahweh occupied both slots. Within Israelite religion, Yahweh's occupation of both top tiers resulted conceptually in two Yahwehs — one invisible, the other visible. At times both speak as characters in the same scene, but more frequently they are virtually interchangeable.

entries.title: Yahweh Refers to Yahweh in the Third Person — Two Yahweh Figures
entries.chapter_or_episode: References to God in the Third Person by Yahweh
entries.content_summary: In certain OT passages, Yahweh refers to Yahweh or 'God' in the third person: Genesis 19:24 — 'Then Yahweh rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire from Yahweh out of heaven' (one Yahweh on the ground, another Yahweh in heaven). Amos 4:11 — 'I overthrew some of you, as when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah... declares Yahweh' (Yahweh speaks of 'God' as though referring to a distinct person). These passages directly support the two-powers-in-heaven theology.
entries.content_detail: Jewish thinkers in the Second Temple period (516 BC-AD 70) were quite familiar with these patterns and did not consider them a violation of monotheism. Yahweh was alone at the top of the heavenly host, albeit in two forms. Jewish theologians devoted much speculation to identifying the second Yahweh — candidates included divinized humans (Adam, Abraham, Moses), exalted angels (Gabriel, Michael), and intermediate figures (Philo's 'the Word'). The early Christians altered these speculations by identifying the second power with Jesus. It wasn't until the second century AD that Jewish authorities declared the 'two powers' teaching heresy.
